items.q: What is the 'race to the bottom' critique of globalisation?
items.correct: The argument that TNCs play countries off against each other, relocating to those with lowest wages, weakest regulation and lowest taxes — pressuring governments to cut standards to attract investment.
items.misconception: The race to the bottom means TNCs compete with each other for the lowest prices.
items.random_wrong: Race to the bottom is when companies offshore work to mines and underground factories.
items.why: It's about regulatory competition between states, not price competition between firms. It explains why LICs may keep labour and environmental rules low — competing for FDI rather than improving workers' conditions.

items.q: What happens at a constructive (divergent) plate boundary?
items.correct: Plates move apart, magma rises to fill the gap and solidifies, forming new oceanic crust — for example at the Mid-Atlantic Ridge.
items.misconception: At constructive boundaries the plates collide and build mountains.
items.random_wrong: At constructive boundaries the plates slide past each other sideways.
items.why: Students confuse 'constructive' with 'collision'. Constructive means new crust is constructed; the plates move apart, not together. Sliding sideways is a conservative/transform boundary.

items.q: Why do people move from rural to urban areas in LICs?
items.correct: A combination of push factors (rural poverty, lack of services, agricultural change) and pull factors (perceived jobs, education, healthcare) — though reality often disappoints.
items.misconception: Because cities in poor countries are always better than the countryside.
items.random_wrong: Because the government forces them to move to cities.
items.why: Migrants often end up in shanty towns worse than what they left. The decision is based on perception of opportunity, not guaranteed improvement — a key nuance students miss.

items.q: What does a wide-based pyramid usually indicate?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: High fertility. “Ageing population” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which age range is normally considered 'working-age' in dependency ratios?
items.why: It's 15-64. “18-65” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: If a country has 30 children, 60 working-age and 20 elderly per 100, what is its dependency ratio (per 100)?
items.why: The real figure is 83. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
